  • Download Rmd

Correlation and regression analysis example

Problem

Load the data set cats from the “MASS” library, which contains information on various anatomical features of house cats.

```r
library(MASS)
data(cats)

<!-- rnb-source-end -->


<!-- rnb-output-end -->

<!-- rnb-chunk-end -->


<!-- rnb-text-begin -->


"Bwt" is the body weight in kilograms, "Hwt" is the heart weight in grams, and "Sex" should be obvious. There are no missing values in any of the variables.

* Have a look at the data set.
* Plot a data (scatterplot of Bwt and Hwt)
* Are "Bwt" and "Hwt" correlated? Test for correlation - use the Pearson correlation coefficient.
* Does "Hwt" depends on "Bwt"? Plot the regression line and then use appropriate plots to check for model assumptions.
* Comment on the diagnostic plots.

# Solution


<!-- rnb-text-end -->


<!-- rnb-chunk-begin -->

<!-- rnb-source-begin eyJkYXRhIjoiYGBgclxuYGBgclxuaGVhZChjYXRzKVxuYGBgXG5gYGAifQ== -->

```r
```r
head(cats)

```r
```r
cor(cats$Bwt, cats$Hwt) # the correlation coefficient is large (a value of 1 would indicates that the variables are positively linearly related and the scatter plot falls almost along a straight line with positive slope)

<!-- rnb-source-end -->


<!-- rnb-output-end -->

<!-- rnb-output-begin eyJkYXRhIjoiWzFdIDAuODA0MTI3NFxuIn0= -->

```r
```r
cor.test(cats$Bwt, cats$Hwt)

data: cats\(Bwt and cats\)Hwt t = 16.119, df = 142, p-value < 2.2e-16 alternative hypothesis: true correlation is not equal to 0 95 percent confidence interval: 0.7375682 0.8552122 sample estimates: cor 0.8041274

```r
# test for normality
shapiro.test(cats$Hwt)


    Shapiro-Wilk normality test

data:  cats$Hwt
W = 0.96039, p-value = 0.0003654
```r
mod <- lm(Hwt ~ Bwt, data=cats)
summary(mod)

<!-- rnb-source-end -->


<!-- rnb-output-end -->

Coefficients: Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)
(Intercept) -0.3567 0.6923 -0.515 0.607
Bwt 4.0341 0.2503 16.119 <2e-16 *** — Signif. codes: 0 ‘’ 0.001 ‘’ 0.01 ‘’ 0.05 ‘.’ 0.1 ‘ ’ 1

Residual standard error: 1.452 on 142 degrees of freedom Multiple R-squared: 0.6466, Adjusted R-squared: 0.6441 F-statistic: 259.8 on 1 and 142 DF, p-value: < 2.2e-16

```r
plot(cats$Bwt, cats$Hwt)
abline(coef(mod), col=\red\)
par(mfrow=c(2,2))
